title: Fitting plasterboard to a stud wall
subtitle: Hang and fix sheets to a timber stud frame ready for skimming
excerpt: How to hang plasterboard on a timber stud frame: marking stud positions, cutting sheets to size, fixing with screws, taping joints with scrim tape, and applying a jointing compound base coat ready for skimming.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Dry Lining syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: A panel of gypsum plaster sandwiched between two layers of paper. Standard thickness is 12.5 mm for walls and 9.5 mm for ceilings. The grey face (tapered edge) goes outward for a dry-line finish; the ivory face (square edge) goes outward for a skim-plaster finish.

A vertical timber member in a stud partition wall. Typically 75 x 50 mm CLS softwood at 400 mm or 600 mm centres. All sheet edges must fall on a stud for support; sheets should not span between studs unsupported at their edges.

A short horizontal timber fixed between the studs in a stud partition, typically at mid-height, to stiffen the frame and provide a fixing point for horizontal sheet edges and for fittings such as radiator brackets and wall-mounted units.

content.text: Plasterboard is fixed to a stud frame with plasterboard screws, not nails, on any domestic job where the finish is to be skimmed or tape-and-jointed. Screws pulled below the surface leave a clean dimple; a nail tends to tear the paper face and cause a bump. Every sheet edge must bear on a
; a sheet edge unsupported in mid-span will flex and crack the finish.
sheets in 12.5 mm or 15 mm thickness, plus plasterboard screws (32 mm for 12.5 mm board), a utility knife, a straightedge, a rasp for trimming edges, scrim tape, and jointing compound.
Step 1, mark stud positions on the floor and ceiling.
Mark the stud centres on the floor plate and ceiling plate with pencil before lifting any sheets. The marks will guide screw placement once the board is up and the studs are hidden.

Measure the height from floor to ceiling minus 10 mm (the 5 mm gap at each end prevents the board from bridging any movement in the floor or ceiling). Score the face with a utility knife along a straightedge, snap the board backward, then cut through the paper on the back face. Trim cut edges with a rasp if needed.

Lift the board into position. Screw to every stud across the board width, at 150 mm centres at edges and 230 mm centres in the field. Each screw should be dimpled just below the paper surface, not so deep that the paper tears. All sheet edges must share a stud with the adjacent sheet; do not leave an edge in free air.

Apply joint scrim tape over every joint between sheets, pressing firmly to the face. Apply a thin base coat of jointing compound over the scrim tape and over all screw dimples, feathering the edges outward. Allow to dry overnight. Lightly sand and apply a second, wider coat, feathering out further. Once dry, the surface is ready for a skim plaster finish.
A screw that breaks through the paper face loses its grip entirely. Tighten just enough to dimple the paper without tearing it.
A butt joint (where two square-cut edges meet) in the middle of a span is a weak point. Add a nogging behind every horizontal butt joint.
